case_studies.project_title: App prototype for integrating online student services at Universidad Técnica de Manabí.

content.title: Overview
content.name: overview
content.description:
Back in 2022, while at my last semester at university, I initiated the design of a mobile app as my final graduation project. It was meant to help fellow students manage academic tasks more efficiently. The concept aimed to streamline access to key university services such as course schedules, enrollment, academic requests, and notifications. 
Although the concept was never taken into development, the project became an important early exploration of user-centered product design, information architecture, and mobile interaction workflows.

content.title: Problem
content.name: problem_overview
content.description:
Many students (myself included) found the university’s online systems fragmented and unintuitive, especially on mobile. Key actions like enrolling in courses, viewing schedules, or making official requests often required navigating outdated web portals. 
I wanted to explore how a unified mobile app could improve this experience by centralizing these actions in a clean, intuitive interface.

1.content: The app was organized into five primary sections:

3.content: Note: Tutorias was not in the scope of the project development, but it was defined.

1.content: I created user flows to map out common journeys.

3.content: These flows helped define the screen structure and interaction logic for the prototype.

dynamic_content.title: Design Process
dynamic_content.name: design_process
dynamic_content.description: This project followed a structured and iterative design process, beginning with low-fidelity sketches and evolving into a polished high-fidelity prototype. My goal was to create a user-centered experience that balanced clarity, functionality, and visual appeal.

dynamic_content.title: From Wireframes to Interface
dynamic_content.name: prototyping
dynamic_content.description:
To kick off the design phase, I translated early research and ideas into rough visual concepts. These wireframes helped define the layout, core features, and navigation logic. Working in low fidelity allowed for quick iterations and feedback without getting caught up in visuals too early. 
 Once the structure was validated, I moved on to crafting a detailed high-fidelity prototype using Figma. The focus here was on visual hierarchy, consistency, and interaction flow, ensuring that the design aligned with usability best practices and real-world functionality.

steps.title: Login
steps.description: Login screen allowing students to securely access their account.

steps.title: Home
steps.description: Central dashboard with a warming wecome note and quick access to key sections like Requests, Reports, and Tutoring.

steps.title: Reportes
steps.description: Reports hub where students can access Certificates, Schedules, Grades, Academic History, and Career Curriculums.

steps.title: Certificados
steps.description: Section to request various official university certificates (e.g. enrollment, ID, grades).

steps.title: Horarios
steps.description: Weekly course schedule view, helping students plan and manage their academic time.

steps.title: Calificaciones
steps.description: Section where students can review their grades for each subject by period.

steps.title: Solicitudes
steps.description: Allows students to submit academic requests (e.g. subject change, enrollment assistance, general procedures).

1.content: While this was an early project in my journey, it was a key learning moment. I got hands-on experience with:

content: Designing components and building scalable UI systems in Figma
Working with design constraints and prioritizing usability

1.content: Although this prototype was never built or submitted, it reflects my early passion for problem-solving through design. It helped me develop empathy for users and laid the foundation for how I approach UI/UX in my current projects as a Full-Stack Software Engineer.

case_studies.project_title: Real-Time Interactive Trivia Platform for Sports Streaming

content.title: Overview
content.name: overview
content.description:
This project involved designing and building a real-time interactive trivia system for a sports streaming environment. The goal was to create an engaging second-screen experience where viewers could participate in trivia games while watching a live broadcast.

The platform consists of two main components: a video rendering engine that generates trivia segments for broadcast and a mobile-first web application where users participate in synchronized trivia sessions. The system integrates with the streaming platform using WebSockets to coordinate gameplay, leaderboards, and player sessions in real time.

content.title: Problem
content.name: problem_overview
content.description:
Sports streaming platforms often struggle to maintain viewer engagement during breaks in live events. Traditional advertising fills some of this space, but it rarely creates meaningful interaction.

The goal of this project was to design a system that could transform passive viewers into active participants by allowing them to play synchronized trivia games while watching the broadcast.

1.content: The platform was designed as a real-time distributed system connecting three main components:

content: A video rendering engine that generates trivia segments
A backend service responsible for game state and synchronization
A mobile-first web application designed for synchronized second-screen participation during live sports broadcasts

3.content: The backend integrates with the streaming platform through WebSockets, allowing the broadcast and the mobile application to remain synchronized during gameplay.

content: Maintaining synchronization between the broadcast and player devices with minimal latency
Handling concurrent player participation during live trivia sessions
Ensuring reliable WebSocket reconnection behavior during unstable mobile network conditions
Coordinating dynamic trivia content generation with broadcast timing constraints
Designing frontend flows optimized for second-screen mobile interaction during live events

1.content: Trivia sessions are synchronized between the broadcast and the mobile application using WebSockets.

2.content: When a trivia segment starts in the broadcast, the backend sends real-time events to connected players, triggering the start of the quiz session on their devices.

1.content: A key component of the system is the video rendering engine built using Remotion. This engine dynamically generates trivia videos used during broadcast segments.

2.content: Content such as questions, facts, and quotes can be programmatically rendered into video segments, allowing the platform to generate new trivia content quickly.

dynamic_content.title: Mobile Gameplay Experience
dynamic_content.name: mobile_app
dynamic_content.description: The mobile web application allows users to participate in trivia sessions synchronized with the broadcast. Players answer questions in real time and compete on leaderboards during the game.

steps.title: Game Lobby
steps.description: Players join the trivia session and wait for the broadcast to start the next question.

steps.title: Trivia Question
steps.description: Players answer multiple-choice trivia questions synchronized with the broadcast.

steps.title: Leaderboard
steps.description: Real-time leaderboard showing top players based on score and response time.

steps.title: Player History
steps.description: Players can review previous trivia sessions and their performance over time.

content: Synchronizing trivia gameplay with a live broadcast
Managing real-time player sessions and leaderboards
Designing a scalable WebSocket communication layer
Generating dynamic video content using a rendering engine
Ensuring the mobile web experience performed reliably during live sessions

1.content: This project was a significant opportunity to design and implement a production-scale interactive system. It required combining frontend development, backend services, real-time communication, and media generation workflows.

2.content: Working on this system strengthened my experience with real-time architectures and interactive product design, particularly in environments where multiple systems must remain synchronized.

case_studies.project_title: Media rendering workflow system for orchestrating asynchronous video generation pipelines.

content.title: Overview
content.name: overview
content.description:
This project is a media rendering workflow system designed to automate asynchronous video generation using Remotion and Node.js. The platform was built as part of a larger internal ecosystem and focuses on orchestrating render jobs, managing render lifecycle states, and providing operational visibility through a lightweight dashboard.

The system exposes backend endpoints that enqueue rendering jobs into a MySQL-backed queue workflow, where a worker process continuously monitors pending jobs, evaluates render eligibility, processes renders, and manages retry and cancellation flows.

content.title: Problem
content.name: problem_overview
content.description:
The platform originated from the need to automate the generation of customized marketing-oriented video assets while maintaining operational control over rendering workflows.

Instead of processing renders synchronously during user interactions, the system required a backend workflow capable of queuing render requests, processing them asynchronously, tracking render states, handling failures, and exposing operational visibility through APIs and dashboard tooling.

1.content: The application was structured as a monorepo-based system composed of multiple applications and shared packages.

content: Express.js backend exposing rendering and operational endpoints
Worker loop process responsible for evaluating and processing render jobs
Remotion-based rendering engine for generating dynamic video compositions
Next.js operational dashboard used to monitor render status and manage render operations
Shared packages organized through npm workspaces

3.content: The rendering infrastructure was intentionally designed to operate within a locally integrated operational environment rather than a distributed cloud architecture.

1.content: Render requests are initiated through backend endpoints that create persistent queue entries within MySQL.

2.content: A worker loop continuously polls the database for eligible render jobs and transitions them through different lifecycle states during processing.

1.content: The system includes a lightweight operational dashboard built with Next.js for monitoring render jobs and reviewing render lifecycle states.

2.content: Dashboard synchronization was implemented through periodic polling against backend render status endpoints, allowing operational visibility without introducing additional real-time infrastructure complexity.

1.content: The render workflow persisted operational metadata and lifecycle state directly within MySQL to provide visibility and reliability across asynchronous processing operations.

content: Designing an asynchronous rendering workflow without blocking API operations
Persisting queue lifecycle states reliably within the database
Managing retry and cancellation workflows for failed render jobs
Coordinating worker polling and render eligibility evaluation
Structuring the platform as a maintainable monorepo architecture
Providing operational visibility into render processing workflows

1.content: This project became a valuable exploration of asynchronous workflow orchestration, render lifecycle management, and operational tooling design.

2.content: Working on the platform deepened my understanding of backend automation workflows, persistent queue coordination, monorepo application organization, and the practical engineering tradeoffs involved in media rendering infrastructure.
